coredata.dc:title: Constructing novel ternary composites of carbon quantum dots/Bi2MoO6/graphitic nanofibers with tunable band structure and boosted photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Weiya
Wang, Shuhong
Zhou, Qin
Liu, Xin
Chen, Xirong
Yang, Kai
Yu, Changlin
Li, Dan
coredata.dc:description:
Novel ternary carbon quantum dots (CDs)/Bi2MoO6 (BMO)/graphitic carbon nanofibers (GNFs) composites (CDs/BMO/GNFs) were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method and studied for use as photocatalysts. The morphological, structural and physicochemical properties of the as-prepared photocatalysts were examined in detail; that the CDs-decorated BMO particles anchored on the surfaces of modified GNFs to form heterojunctions. The photocatalytic activity of the resultant CDs/BMO/GNFs was superior for the degradation of three different organics, including Rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB) and phenol, as well as the reduction of a heavy metal ion Cr(VI), when compared with the corresponding binary composites (BMO/GNFs and CDs/BMO) and pure BMO. Our study revealed that the photocatalytic performance of ternary composite CDs/BMO/GNFs was significantly affected by the loadings of CDs or/and GNFs in BMO. Especially, the optimized sample C5/BMO/GNF-0.2, which was synthesized with a Bi/C molar ratio of 0.2 and the addition of 5 mL CDs, exhibited the best activity in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB. It was found that 99.4% of RhB was removed after 70-min exposure to the simulated solar light irradiation. This was attributed to the synergistic effects between components (CDs, BMO and GNFs) and heterojunctions, resulting in an efficient spatial separation of the photogenerated electrons and holes. A possible photocatalytic mechanism of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 was proposed that the photogenerated holes,
O2− and
OH radicals contributed to the photocatalysis, which was supported by the radical quenching experiment and electron spin resonance analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi2MoO6
Carbon dots
Graphitic carbon nanofibers
Ternary composites
Photocatalytic removal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Discussion on the enhanced photocatalytic performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of Bi2MoO6 (BMO), modified GNF and BMO/GNFs composites; (b) XRD patterns of C5/BMO and Cx/BMO/GNF-0.2 composites; (c) FT-IR of the BMO, modified GNF, CDs, C5/BMO, BMO/GNFs-0.2 and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2; (d) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore distributions (the insets) of the BMO, C5/BMO, BMO/GNF-0.2 and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2.
SEM images for (a) pristine BMO, (b) modified GNFs, and (c) C5/BMO/CNF-0.2.
TEM images of pristine BMO (a); TEM and HR TEM images of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 (b–d).
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) Bi 4f, (b) Mo 3d, (c) O 1s and (d) C 1s regions for the C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 composite.
(a). UV–vis spectra for the as-prepared samples, (b) Band gap for BMO, C5/BMO, BMO/GNF-0.2, and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 composites.

(A) Photocatalytic degradation of RhB as a function of irradiation time over the as-prepared samples, (B) Degradation efficiency (%) of RhB over the as-prepared samples at irridation time of 70 min ((a) BMO (b) C5/BMO, (c) BMO/GNF-0.1, (d) BMO/GNF-0.2, (e) BMO/GNF-0.4, (f) C3/BMO/GNF-0.2, (g) C5/BMO/GNF-0.2, (h) C7/BMO/GNF-0.2, (i) C9/BMO/GNF-0.2).
Photoactivity of (a) MB, (b) phenol, and reduction of (c) Cr(VI) over as- prepared samples.
(a) Photoluminescence (PL) of BMO, C5/BMO, BMO/GNF-0.2 and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2;
Photocurrent measurements of BMO, BMO/GNF-0.2, C5/BMO and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2.
Effect of different active species scavengers on the degradation of RhB over C5/BMO/GNF-0.2.

ESR signals of (a) DMPO-
OH and (b) DMPO-
O2 − of BMO and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis pathway of the CDs/MBO/GNFs composites.
Photocatalytic reaction mechanism over ternary composite C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 and possible transferred direction and separated behavior of photon-generated carrier.

head.ce:title: Constructing novel ternary composites of carbon quantum dots/Bi2MoO6/graphitic nanofibers with tunable band structure and boosted photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgy and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Metallurgy and Chemical Engineering
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Health, Engineering and Education, Murdoch University, Murdoch 6150, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science, Health, Engineering and Education

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Environmental Science and Engineering, Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Pollution Process and Control, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology, Maoming 525000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Environmental Science and Engineering
Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Pollution Process and Control

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel ternary carbon dots (CDs)/Bi2MoO6 (BMO)/graphitic carbon nanofibers (GNFs) composite exhibited excellent solar-light-driven photocatalytic activity for degradation of organics and reduction of heavy metal ions. This may be explained by the synergistic effects of components (CDs, BMO and GNFs) and heterojunctions, resulting in an improved spatial separation of electrons and holes.

Novel ternary carbon quantum dots (CDs)/Bi2MoO6 (BMO)/graphitic carbon nanofibers (GNFs) composites (CDs/BMO/GNFs) were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method and studied for use as photocatalysts. The morphological, structural and physicochemical properties of the as-prepared photocatalysts were examined in detail; that the CDs-decorated BMO particles anchored on the surfaces of modified GNFs to form heterojunctions. The photocatalytic activity of the resultant CDs/BMO/GNFs was superior for the degradation of three different organics, including Rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB) and phenol, as well as the reduction of a heavy metal ion Cr(VI), when compared with the corresponding binary composites (BMO/GNFs and CDs/BMO) and pure BMO. Our study revealed that the photocatalytic performance of ternary composite CDs/BMO/GNFs was significantly affected by the loadings of CDs or/and GNFs in BMO. Especially, the optimized sample C5/BMO/GNF-0.2, which was synthesized with a Bi/C molar ratio of 0.2 and the addition of 5 mL CDs, exhibited the best activity in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB. It was found that 99.4% of RhB was removed after 70-min exposure to the simulated solar light irradiation. This was attributed to the synergistic effects between components (CDs, BMO and GNFs) and heterojunctions, resulting in an efficient spatial separation of the photogenerated electrons and holes. A possible photocatalytic mechanism of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 was proposed that the photogenerated holes,
O2− and
OH radicals contributed to the photocatalysis, which was supported by the radical quenching experiment and electron spin resonance analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few decades, environmental pollution has aroused wide public attention. Some of pollution was caused by the discharge of dyes and phenols, which are widely used in textile, paint, plastic, agricultural and dyeing industries and thereby are present in the wastewater [1–4]. They are harmful to aquatic environment, even when at a low concentration. For example, Rhodamine B (RhB), which is a representative of xanthene dye, has the property of neurotoxicity, carcinogenicity and teratogenicity to humans and animals. Methylene blue (MB) is less harmful but can cause irritation to the gastrointestinal tract and skin under continuous exposure [5,6]. Phenol is well known for its good chemical stability; it is degraded very slowly by natural processes and highly persistent in the water environment, causing risk to human health and environment because of acute toxicity and carcinogenicity [7,8]. In addition to organic pollutants, toxic heavy metals introduce great threat to aquatic environment [9]. For instance, Cr(VI), which widely exists in the discharge of many industries, is highly toxic and mutagenic [10,11]. As can be seen, it is necessary to eliminate those harmful organic contaminants and heavy metal in the wastewater before its discharge into natural water bodies. Herein, we selected Rhodamine B(RhB), methylene blue (MB), and phenol as the models of organic pollutants and Cr(VI) as the model of heavy metal pollutants to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of our newly developed materials.

Up to now, different methods have been carried out to remove organic and heavy metal pollutants from water [9,12,13]. In particular, semiconductor photocatalysis has become one of the promising solutions, since it can photochemically eliminate those pollutants by utilizing naturally available solar energy [14]. Many semiconductors and their composites have been explored and used as efficient photocatalysts in various applications [15,16], e.g. photodegradation of organic pollutants [17–20], water splitting producing hydrogen [21–23], and treatment of heavy metal ions [10,24]. However, traditional photocatalysts, such as TiO2, can only respond to UV light due to the large band gap energy [25]; this limits their potential by utilizing abundant solar energy. Therefore, to make the best use of solar light, it is desirable to develop novel visible-light-driven semiconductor photocatalysts [17,19,23,26–29]. Bi2MoO6 (BMO) has attracted considerable research interest, owing to its narrow band gap energy (Eg = 2.3–2.7 eV), visible-light absorption, inexpensiveness, and non-toxicity [30,31]. Unfortunately, its use is being largely limited by the rapid recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which leads to a relatively low quantum yield and unsatisfied photocatalytic efficiency. Some efforts have been carried out to resolve this issue, including morphology control [32], element doping [33], and heterojunction design [34,35].

Recently, surface decoration/coupling of semiconductor materials has been utilized as an effective strategy to facilitate the separation of photogenerated carriers and consequently ameliorate the photocatalytic performance [3,15,21,36,37]. Since the photocatalytic reaction usually occurs on the catalyst surface, it is favourable for the photoexcited electrons to transfer from the bulk to the surface and then be trapped by the coupled material on its surface, resulting in the inhibition of fast recombination of electron-hole pairs. The materials in the form of zero-dimensional (0D) quantum dots, such as CdS [38], g-C3N4 [39], graphene [40], and carbon quantum dots [41], have been successfully used for surface decoration of other semiconductors or even for self-decoration [10]. The as-prepared photocatalysts exhibited attractive features, including expanded light adsorption and improved conductivity, thus greatly inhibiting the photogenerated electron-hole recombination and boosting their photocatalytic performances [10]. Among them, carbonquantum dots (CDs), have gained considerable interest, ascribed to their outstanding optical and electronic properties, water solubility, biocompatibility, nontoxicity, cost effectiveness, and so forth [42]. More importantly, CDs possess excellent up-conversion photoluminescence properties (UPCL), which can utilize visible and near-infrared spectrum of sunlight. When was selected as one of the components in the binary composite for photocatalysis, they also had the ability of trapping and transferring the photoinduced electrons from a semiconductor to achieve efficient separation of electron-hole pairs [43]. So far, CDs have been successfully used to design a series of binary composites (e.g. CDs/TiO2 [44], CDs/CdS [45], CDs/NiB [46], and CDs/BMO [41,43]), which have shown alluring advantages in many photocatalytic processes.

As compared with the pure BMO or BMO-based binary composites, the BMO-containing ternary composites exhibited superior photocatalytic activities, thanks to the advantageous features arising from each component [10,39,47,48]. Especially, by integrating heterojunctions in the ternary catalytic systems, such as g-C3N4 quantum dots (CNQDs)/Ag/BMO [39], BMO/CNTs/g-C3N4 [48], g-C3N4/C@BMO [47], and Pt/C@BMO [49], etc., it can largely promote the separation and suppress the recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs on the interfaces of BMO. These ternary composites showed promising improvement in the photocatalytic performance for many applications. For example, Lin et al. fabricated the ternary composite of heterostructured CNQDs/Ag/BMO, which could photocatalytically remove around 100% RhB in 30 min. This was significantly higher than those of binary composites, Ag/BMO (57.5%) and CNQDs/BMO (68.0%), respectively [39].

Considering the attractive characteristics of BMO for photocatalysis as well as efficient electron trapping and transferring property of CDs, the combination of those two materials is of interest for the development of novel composites with enhanced photocatalytic efficiency [41,43]. Especially, in our literature search, it is noted that no work has published on CDs/BMO-containing ternary composite photocatalysts. Therefore, targeting at the design of high-performance CDs/BMO-containing ternary composite photocatalysts, we selected graphitic carbon nanofibers (GNFs) as the third component. GNFs can be obtained from the carbonization of one-dimensional (1D) carbon nanofibers (CNFs) by increasing temperature [50]. They were found to provide good charge transfer paths for efficient capture and transport of photogenerated electrons because of their excellent conductivity [48,51]. So far there has been no research reported on the GNFs-integrated ternary composites for utilization as photocatalysts to remove organics or to reduce heavy metal ions in water.

Herein, we synthesized a series of CDs/BMO/GNFs by a facile hydrothermal method and studied their photocatalytic application for the removal of different organic pollutants and heavy metal ions. To the best of our knowledge, the CDs/BMO/GNFs ternary composites were synthesized and investigated for the first time. The amounts of CDs and GNFs added during the fabrication of CDs/BMO/GNFs ternary composites were varied and optimized towards high photocatalytic performance. The results showed a significant enhancement in the degradation of RhB, MB and phenol, as well as the reduction of Cr(VI) by using our newly developed ternary CDs/BMO/GNFs composites. The possible mechanisms were also explored and presented in this paper.

Graphitic carbon nanofibers (GNFs) with the length of 10–20 μm were purchased from Beijing Dk Nano technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, C6H5Na3O7·H2O, HNO3, H2SO4, glucose, Rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB), phenol, and K2Cr2O7 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents are analytical grade and were used directly without further purification.

GNFs were pre-treated according to the literature with some modifications [52]. This pre-treatment could endow functional groups of OH and COOH favouring the formation of heterojunctions. 1.0 g of GNFs were dispersed in 40 mL of the solution which was consisting of concentrated HNO3 and H2SO4 (volume ratio 1:3) for 30 min under ultrasonication. The resulting suspension was then filtrated and washed thoroughly with distilled water, followed by drying at 80 °C overnight. The obtained black powders were collected for further use.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal process based on the Ref. [41]. Typically, 0.10 g of glucose was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized (DI) water under stirring. 80 mL of the as-prepared solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm to remove the solids; the resultant solution contained CDs.

The synthetic process of CDs/BMO/GNFs composites via a facile hydrothermal method is shown in Scheme 1 . Typically, 1.08 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 31.25 mL of 10% HNO3 solution, followed by adding 0.58 g (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O. The resultant solution was kept stirring at 40 °C for 10 min, after which 1.44 g C6H5Na3O7·H2O was added. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 5.0 using ammonia solution (25–28 wt%), followed by adding 1.75 mL glycol. Afterwards, 0.13 g of modified GNFs (a Bi/C molar ratio of 0.2) was added, followed by stirring for 1 h at 80 °C. A desired amount of CDs solution was then introduced into the mixture, which was stirred for another 1 h and then heated in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 150 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the prepared products were filtrated and washed for three times with DI water and ethanol respectively, and dried at 60 °C. The obtained solid product was heated in a tube furnace at 400 °C for 4 h (heating rate of 5 °C min−1). The resulting composite was named as Cx/BMO/CNF-0.2, in which “x = 3, 5, 7, 9” was corresponding to the volume of the added CDs solution (3 mL, 5 mL, 7 mL and 9 mL, respectively).

The composites of BMO/GNFs were synthesized following the same procedures but without the addition of CDs solution. The resulting products were named as BMO/CNF-y (y = 0.4, 0.2, and 0.1, corresponding to the Bi/C molar ratio of 0.4, 0.2, and 0.1). For comparison, CDs/BMO (C5/BMO) was prepared with 5 mL CDs solution but without the addition of GNFs. Pure BMO was also prepared using the same method without adding GNFs and CDs.

The morphology and microstructures were examined by a scanning electron microscopy (SEM; FEG250, FEI company) in a secondary electron scattering mode at 20 kV and transmission electron microscopy (TEM; F20, FEI company) with accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The crystal phases were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8-advance) using Cu Kα (λ = 0.15418 Å) radiation in the 2θ range of 10–80° at a scan rate of 4° min−1. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of the samples were measured using nitrogen adsorption/desorption at 77 K over an automatic analyzer (ASAP 2020). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were investigated with a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrometer; the sample was pressed by a KBr disk preparation apparatus. Raman spectroscopy was performed on a Micro-Raman Spectrometer (inVia, Renishaw). The surface chemical compositions of Bi, Mo, O and C were analyzed by X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALab 250 Xi) using Mg Ka (1253.6 eV) X-rays as the excitation source. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) diffusion reflectance spectra were measured with a SHIMADZU UV-2550 spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as background at room temperature to determine the band gap of the materials. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the samples were analyzed on a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4500, Japan). The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were recorded on an electron spin resonance spectrometer (Bruker ER200-SLC, Germany) with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO: 50 mM, 0.2 mL). The photoelectrochemical measurements were carried out on a Chenhua CHI660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai, China) with a standard three-electrode cell and 0.5 mol/L Na2SO4 as supporting electrolyte. The films of the samples were fabricated on FTO conductive glass as working electrode, along with KCl-saturated calomel electrode as reference electrode and graphite electrode as counter electrode. A 300 W Xe lamp (PLS-SXE300, Perfect Light Company, Beijing, China) was employed as the light source.

Photocatalytic activities of the samples were evaluated for the degradation of three different organics, i.e. Rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB) and phenol, as well as the reduction of heavy metal ion, i.e. Cr(VI). A 300 W Xe lamp was used as light source to simulate solar light. In each experiment, 80 mg of photocatalyst was added in 80 mL of 10 mg/L aqueous solution containing RhB, MB, phenol, or Cr(VI). Before light irradiation, the mixture was stirred in the dark for 30 min to establish the adsorption–desorption equilibrium between the model pollutant and photocatalyst. After that, the mixture was irradiated under the simulated solar light for 70–90 min. During the reaction, ∼3 mL of the solution was sampled at given a given period of time and centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst. The concentration of organic was monitored by measuring the maximum absorbance on a UV–vis spectrophotometer; whilst the concentration of Cr(VI) was determined by using diphenylcarbazide as a color agent and measuring the absorbance at 540 nm on a UV–vis spectrophotometer [53].

The reusability of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 was evaluated by recycling and using it for five cycles of RhB photodegradation. After each use in 90-min photocatalytic reaction, C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 was recycled by centrifugation, washed with distilled water and dried at 80 °C overnight for reuse.

The XRD patterns of BMO, modified GNFs and three different BMO/GNFs composites are shown in Fig. 1 a; whilst the XRD patterns of the CDs/BMO/GNFs composites (Cx/BMO/GNF-0.2) and C5/BMO are demonstrated in Fig. 1b. As can be seen, the XRD pattern of the modified GNFs shows a sharp and strong intensity peak at around 2θ = 26° (Fig. 1a), which is attributed to the (0 0 2) plane of the graphitic carbon in GNFs [54]. A low intensity of graphitic characteristic peak at 2θ = 26° can also be found in the samples of BMO/GNFs composites (BMO/GNF-0.1, BMO/GNF-0.2, and BMO/GNF-0.4). On the other side, the samples of BMO/GNFs composites (BMO/GNF-0.1, BMO/GNF-0.2, and BMO/GNF-0.4) display similar diffraction peaks at 2θ = 28.2°, 32.6°, 46.8°, and 55.4° to BMO, which can be indexed to the (1 3 1), (0 0 2), (0 6 2)/(2 6 0), and (3 3 1)/(1 3 3) planes of the orthorhombic phase of Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS No. 76-2388). This suggests the formation of BMO/GNFs heterostructures. In Fig. 1b, the XRD patterns of the Cx/BMO/GNF-0.2 composites show no obvious difference as compared to that of BMO/GNF-0.2, probably due to the small amount of CDs introduced. Such findings were also reported in other similar systems [41,43]. The XRD pattern of C5/BMO shows similar peaks to that of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2, but without the presence of graphitic characteristic peak (2θ = ∼26°).

The FT-IR spectra of BMO, CDs, modified GNFs, BMO/GNF-0.2, C5/BMO and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 are demonstrated in Fig. 1c. For the modified GNFs, the broad peak at about 3444 cm−1 and the sharp peak at 1633 cm−1 correspond to the surface adsorbed H2O and OH groups [52], respectively. The OH groups may be derived from the mixed acid treatment of GNFs. The FT-IR spectrum of BMO clearly displays several characteristic peaks, the band at 452 cm−1 is ascribed to the BiO stretching; whilst those at 573, 727, and 838 cm−1 can be assigned to the MoO stretching vibration [43]. The characteristic peaks of BMO and modified GNFs are also observed in the spectra of BMO/GNF-0.2, proving the existence of BMO and GNFs in the samples of BMO/GNF-0.2. In the FTIR spectrum of CDs, the broad absorption bands at 3448 and 1647 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of –OH and CO groups [43]; the peaks at 1396 and 1129 cm−1 are probably due to the stretch of carboxylate group and asymmetrical vibration of COC group in CDs [55]. The main characteristic peaks of CDs and BMO are both observed in the sample C5/BMO, indicating the successful coupling of CDs and BMO. Especially, in the FTIR spectrum of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2, main characteristic peaks of CDs, modified GNFs and BMO are all observed but at weaker intensities, suggesting the coexistence of CDs, GNFs and BMO in the ternary C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 composite.

The Raman spectra of BMO, BMO/GNF-0.2 and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 are shown in Fig. S1. The spectrum of BMO shows vibration modes in the regions of 200–500 cm−1 and 700–900 cm−1 [41], where the peak centering at 313 cm−1 is resulted from the bending modes of MoO octahedra and the stretching and bending modes of Bi-O polyhedral [47,56]. The peaks at 1320 and 1575 cm−1 in the spectrum of BMO/GNF-0.2 are ascribed to the D band and G band of the carbonaceous materials [50]. They can also be observed in that of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2, while the D band shows a slight shift. On the other side, the characteristic peaks of BMO are seen in the spectrum of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2; however, their intensities are weakened. This further confirms the presence of BMO as well as the interactions between BMO, GNFs and CDs in the sample C5/BMO/GNF-0.2.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of BMO, BMO/GNF-0.2, C5/BMO and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 are shown in Fig. 1d. Their corresponding BET specific surface areas and pore volumes are listed in Table S1, as well as the other as-prepared samples. The BET specific surface areas of BMO, C5/BMO and BMO/GNF-0.2 are recorded as 7.83, 5.05 and 7.48 m2 g−1, respectively; while that of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 remarkably increases to 20.72 m2 g−1. Compared with that of BMO, the surface areas of Cx/BMO/GNF-0.2 samples are greater, in the range of 12.76–23.38 m2 g−1 (Table S1), probably due to the coupling of both CDs and GNFs. The greater surface area would favour the contact between the photocatalyst and pollutants, and in turn improve the photocatalytic performance [39].

In Figs. 2 a and 3 a, BMO shows the morphology of agglomerates with a size ranging from ∼0.7 to 1.5 μm, which seem to be assembled by a number of BMO nanosheets. The TEM and HR-TEM images (Fig. S2a-b) of BMO indicate that the lattice fringe of BMO crystallite is 0.316 nm, corresponding to the (1 3 1) crystal plane of Bi2MoO6 [43]. The diameters of the modified GNFs are between 50 and 100 nm, which exhibit relatively smooth surfaces (Figs. 2b and S2c). The corresponding HR-TEM image (Fig. S2d) shows a fringe spacing of 0.339 nm, corresponding to the lattice space (0 0 2) for graphite. The TEM and HR-TEM images of CDs (Fig. S3) show that the average diameter of CDs is ∼8 nm; the lattice spacing of 0.332 nm is assigned to the (0 0 2) crystal plane of CDs. Figs. 2c and 3b show the morphology of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2, that many particles of CD-decorated BMO, which are obviously smaller than pure BMO, anchored on the surface of modified GNFs. The TEM images at high magnification (Fig. 3c; and Fig. 3d which reflects the rectangle area in Fig. 3c) reveal the ternary heterostructure of CDs/BMO/GNFs. The lattice spacings of 0.327 and 0.276 nm correspond to the crystal planes of (0 4 1) and (0 0 2) in BMO; while that of 0.332 nm is ascribed to the graphitic lattice space (0 0 2) in CDs, respectively. Therefore, accompanied with the aforementioned XRD and FTIR characterization, the TEM results strongly support the coexistence of CDs, GNFs, and BMO in the sample of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2.

Fig. 4 shows the high resolution XPS spectra of Bi 4f, Mo 3d, O 1s, and C 1s regions in C5/BMO/GNF-0.2. Fig. S4 demonstrates the XPS survey spectrum, that Bi, Mo, C, and O elements exist on the surface of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2. As can be seen from Fig. 4a, the binding energy located at 159.6 and 164.5 eV is attributed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of the Bi3+ ions; whilst those at around 232.8 and 235.9 eV in Fig. 4b can be ascribed to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 of the Mo6+ ions, respectively [32,47]. As shown in the O 1s region (Fig. 4c), the peak of binding energy centering at 530.7 eV can be fitted into two peaks at 530.5 and 531.1 eV, corresponding to the Bi-O and O-H groups on the surface of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 [51]. The C 1s spectral peaks at 284.1 and 284.6 eV might be assigned to the CC bond from CDs and GNFs. On the other side, the peak at 288.9 eV is the characteristic of carboxyl carbon (OCO) [43], which should be originated from modified GNFs and CDs.

The optical properties of the as-prepared samples were studied by the UV–vis DRS measurement. In Fig. 5 a, BMO shows light absorption property from the UV light region to the visible-light region, with the absorption edge at around 466 nm. A significant enhancement of absorption, especially in the visible-light region (>400 nm), is seen in the sample of BMO/GNF-0.2; it might be largely attributed to the light absorbing feature of carbon materials themselves. Similar result was found in the other carbon-integrated composite photocatalyst [57]. As seen in Fig. S5a, all of the BMO/GNFs samples exhibit obvious red-shift as compared with BMO; this is beneficial to boost their photocatalytic activities under visible light. In contrast, the sample C5/BMO shows almost the same adsorption as BMO in the range >550 nm (Fig. 5), whilst an enhanced absorption intensity in the UV light region is observed, owing to the presence of CDs. With the addition of 5 mL CDs solution and proper GNFs (Bi/C molar ratio = 0.2), the UV–vis spectrum of the resulting C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 sample exhibits a significant red-shift of the absorption edge, which is expect to improve the utilization of the solar spectrum. Interestingly, the similar curve is seen for C3/BMO/GNF-0.2, C7/BMO/GNF-0.2, or C9/BMO/GNF-0.2, despite that lower adsorption intensity being observed in the region of >450 nm. The colors of BMO (yellow), C5/BMO (light yellow), BMO/GNF-0.2 (dark army green) and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 (army green) are shown in the insets of Fig. 5a. These color changes would be derived from the integration of CDs and/or GNFs into BMO.

The optical band gap energy (Eg) of BMO, BMO/GNF-0.2, C5/BMO and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 was estimated by plotting the curves of (αhν)2 vs hν [41], as shown in Fig. 5b; the Eg values of all the as-prepared samples are summarized in Table S1. Additionally, Eg of the BMO/GNFs samples is shown in Fig. S5b. As compared with the pristine BMO (2.78 eV), the Eg values of BMO/GNFs decrease in the order of BMO/GNF-0.4 (2.70 eV) > BMO/GNF-0.1 (2.66 eV) > BMO/GNF-0.2 (2.64 eV). Obviously, Eg of BMO/GNF-0.2 is the narrowest among BMO/GNFs. The Eg values of ternary Cx/BMO/GNF-0.2 composites (x = 3, 5, 7, 9) are 2.77, 2.27, 2.73 and 2.67 eV (Table S1), respectively. This result indicates that the band gap of CDs/BMO/GNFs can be tuned by adjusting the composition of the ternary photocatalyst. Notably, among all the as-prepared samples, the Eg value of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 (2.27 eV) is the lowest, suggesting a promoted light absorption capacity in a wider spectral range of solar light. It is expected to contribute a positive impact on its photocatalytic activity.

The photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples were evaluated by examining their use in the photodegradation of RhB, as shown in Fig. 6 . The self-degradation of RhB under 70-min light irradiation was negligible (only 5.6%). We found that the modified GNFs had no photocatalytic activity, although a good adsorptive removal of RhB (∼60%) was observed. In Fig. 6, the removal rate of RhB over BMO/GNFs samples is in the order: BMO/GNF-0.2 (65.3%) > BMO/GNF-0.1 (63.4%) > BMO/GNF-0.4 (33.3%), while approximately 32% RhB is removed when using BMO after 70-min photodegradation. Thus, all of the BMO/GNFs composites exhibit enhanced photocatalytic activity as compared to BMO; it may be explained by the formation of heterojunctions between BMO and GNFs. Moreover, their differences in the optical properties and Eg as revealed in Fig. S5a-b, that in the order of BMO/GNF-0.4 (2.70 eV) > BMO/GNF-0.1 (2.66 eV) > BMO/GNF-0.2 (2.64 eV), support the change of RhB photodegradation rates over the BMO/GNFs samples. Thus, the optimum loading of BMO in the BMO/GNFs composites is suggested as the Bi/C molar ratio of 0.2. With the addition of CDs solution from 3 mL to 9 mL, the photodegradation efficiency of resulting ternary Cx/BMO/GNF-0.2 decreases in the order: C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 > C3/BMO/GNF-0.2 > C7/BMO/GNF-0.2 > C9/BMO/GNF-0.2. This suggests that the amount of CDs introduced during the synthesis of ternary CDs/BMO/GNF composite has a significant effect on the photocatalytic activity of the resultant catalyst. The increasing addition of CDs may cause the the reunion of excess CDs on the surfaces of BMO; it may subsequently weaken the light absorption ability of BMO and in turn lower the photodegradation efficiency of CDs/BMO/GNF [26,41]. This can be confirmed by their UV–vis spectra (Fig. 5a), that there is a red-shift of absorption edge for C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 as compared with those with higher loadings of CDs, e.g. C7/BMO/GNF-0.2 and C9/BMO/GNF-0.2. This is believed to enhance the utilization of visible light in the solar spectrum. Therefore, it is noted that the sample of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 exhibits the best photocatalytic activity with RhB removal rate of 99.4%. This is also significantly higher than that of binary composite C5/BMO (43.4%) or BMO/GNF-0.2 (65.3%).

The UV–vis absorption spectral changes of RhB over C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 are shown in Fig. S6. As the reaction proceeds, the intensity of the RhB absorption peaks decreases gradually and almost disappears in 70 min. The kinetics of the samples were fitted by using the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Eq. (1)) and the relevant parameters are summarized in Table S2 [12]:
(1)
- ln ( c /
c 0
) = k t
where t is the reaction time, C0 and C are the initial concentration of the pollutant and the concentration of the pollutant after a given period of time, and k is the pseudo-first-order rate constant, respectively.

The rate constant k of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 is 0.035 min−1, which is increased by 7.1, 4.1 and 5.4 times, as compared to those of pure BMO, BMO/GNF-0.2 and C5/BMO (Table S2). This further reveals the promising activity of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 for use in the RhB photodegradation.

In order to further evaluate the photocatalytic performance of CDs/BMO/GNF-0.2, other organic pollutants (e.g. MB and phenol) and heavy metal ion (e.g. Cr(VI)) were also selected for study. Fig. S7d-f show the temporal UV–vis absorption spectral changes of MB, phenol and Cr(VI) in the solution when using C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 under the simulated solar light irradiation. The intensities of the characteristic absorption peaks for MB, phenol and Cr(VI), which locate at 664, 270 and 540 nm, decrease gradually. Among the samples of Cx/BMO/GNFs-0.2, C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 shows superior photocatalytic activity, removing ∼75%, 85% and 77% MB, phenol and Cr(VI) after 90-min exposure to the simulated solar light illumination (Fig. 7 a–c). The corresponding pseudo-first-order rate constants of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 are 0.0118, 0.0215, and 0.00975 min−1, respectively (Fig. S7a-c and Table S3). It is notable that the removal rates (and the corresponding pseudo-first-order rate constants) of MB, phenol, and Cr(VI) using C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 are significantly greater than those with the use of BMO, C5/BMO or BMO/GNF-0.2 under the same experimental conditions. Therefore, our results imply that the use of ternary composite by integrating CDs, BMO and GNFs is an effective approach to achieve the enhancement of photocatalytic efficiency. The sample of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 possesses a good capability for recycle and reuse, largely retaining RhB removal rate at around 90% after 5 cycles of use (Fig. S8). Fig. S9 shows the XRD pattern of fresh C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 and that after 3 and 5 runs of use in the photocatalytic reaction. There was no significant change in the XRD patterns, further suggesting the good stability of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion on the enhanced photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: In order to reveal the efficiency of the charge carrier trapping, migration, transferring and separation, Fig. 8 demonstrates the PL spectra of BMO, C5/BMO, BMO/GNF-0.2 and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2. As can be seen, the emission peak of each sample centers at around 400 nm, the intensity of which is in the order: BMO > C5/BMO > BMO/GNF-0.2 > C5/BMO/GNF-0.2. In general, the low PL intensity is ascribed to the reduced recombination rate of photogenerated electron and hole pairs [33,56,58]. Therefore, it can be deduced that the recombination of photogenerated carriers in C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 is most effectively inhibited among all other samples (e.g. BMO, C5/BMO, BMO/GNF-0.2 and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2). It also implies that the construction of ternary C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 composite could be an effective way to promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

The photocurrent measurements of BMO, C5/BMO, BMO/GNF-0.2 and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 are shown in Fig. 9 to further analyze the charge carrier separation capabilities of the samples. The photocurrent density increases in the order of BMO < C5/BMO < BMO/GNF-0.2 < C5/BMO/GNF-0.2, which is in agreement with the order of the observed photocatalytic activity (Fig. 6). The photocurrent of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2, which is much higher than that of pure BMO and their binary catalysts (C5/BMO and BMO/GNF-0.2), indicates that the separation efficiency of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs can be promoted by constructing ternary composite catalyst. These results are coincident with those obtained from the above PL analysis.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to study the resistance of charge transfer and separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [56]. The Nyquist plots of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 and BMO are shown in Fig. S10. C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 shows a smaller resistance arc radius than that of BMO, suggesting a lower charge transfer resistance and higher electron mobility [59].

As clearly can be seen, the above results furnish the valid evidence that the ternary C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 composite possesses an enhanced property of charge carrier transport and photogenerated electron-hole separation. They also strongly support our experimental observation on the improved photocatalytic removal of pollutants when using C5/BMO/GNF-0.2.

To reveal the mechanism governing the enhanced photocatalytic performance of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2, trapping experiments were carried out to detect the main oxidative species (Fig. 10 ). Tert-butyl alcohol (TBA, 1 mM) and triethanolamine (TEA, 1 mM) were utilized as the scavengers of
OH radicals and photogenerated holes (h+), respectively [12]. The role of
O2− radicals was investigated under N2-bubbled experiment [41]. For comparison, the photocatalytic degradation of RhB over C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 without scavengers or N2 bubbling was also included. As can be seen in Fig. 10, the degree of degradation inhibition is in the order of TEA > bubbled N2 > TBA. The presence of TEA causes significant inhibition in the RhB degradation, indicating the indispensable role of photogenerated holes (h+). In contrast, a lower degree of reduction is seen in the degradation after adding TBA; that reveals the participation of some
OH radicals in the photocatalytic reaction. On the other side, the degradation rate is markedly dropped with N2-bubbled into the RhB solution, suggesting that the
O2− radicals produced by dissolved oxygen play a crucial role in the reaction. Thus, it can be inferred that the photogenerated holes,
OH and the
O2− radicals are the main active species, participating the RhB photodegradation.

Fig. 11 shows the ESR spectra of DMPO-
OH and DMPO-
O2 − adducts when using BMO or C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 before and after 10-min light illumination. In Fig. 11a, by using BMO or C5/BMO/GNF-0.2, four characteristic peaks of DMPO-
OH are observed in the solution under light; however no ESR signal of DMPO-
OH is seen in dark. This suggests that
OH is one of active species for photocatalysis. It is notable that the intensity of the characteristic peaks of DMPO-
OH when using C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 is much stronger than that with BMO, indicating more
OH radicals were produced. In Fig. 11b, the characteristic peaks relating to DMPO-
O2 − adduct are observed with the use of BMO and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2. When adding C5/BMO/GNF-0.2, much stronger ESR signals are found than the use of BMO upon light illumination, indicating the generation of more
O2 − radicals. Therefore, the ternary composite C5/BMO/GNF-0.2, synthesized by the integration of CDs, GNFs and BMO, can enable efficient generation of
O2 − and
OH radicals; and subsequently induce higher photocatalytic performance.

The band position of BMO can be calculated by Eqs. (2)–(3) [60,61]:
(2)
E VB
= X -
E
e
+ 0.5
E g
(3)
E CB
=
E VB
-
E g
where Ee is the energy of free electron on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5 eV), X is the absolute electronegativity of the semiconductor (5.55 eV for BMO), and Eg is the band gap of the semiconductor (2.78 eV, Fig. 5b) [62]. Therefore, the potential values of valence band (EVB) and conduction band (ECB) are estimated to be 2.44 and −0.34 eV, respectively.

The flat band potential of BMO and C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 were further investigated via the Mott–Schottky curves (as shown in Fig. S11). It is confirmed that BMO is a n-type semiconductor due to its positive slope of the linear part, which is similar to other reports [12,63]. The flat-band potential position of BMO is −0.53 eV (vs Ag/AgCl, pH = 7) by the extrapolation of the linear portion of the plots; while that of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 shifts toward more positive direction (−0.096 eV (vs Ag/AgCl, pH = 7)). The standard band potential of BMO is calculated as −0.31 eV (vs. NHE, pH = 7), according to the following Eq. (4) [10]:
(4)
E
vs
NHE
= E
vs
Ag / AgCl
+ 0.22
V .

Based on the value of Eg (2.78 eV) obtained from the UV–Vis DRS (Fig. 5b), the potentials of CB and VB for BMO are approximately −0.31 and 2.47 eV (vs NHE at pH = 7), which are consistent with the above calculation. Herein, the CB potential of BMO (−0.31 eV) is more negative than E0(O2/
O2 −) (−0.046 eV vs. NHE) [43], indicating that it is possible to reduce dissolved O2 to produce
O2 − radicals. In addition, the electrons from CB of BMO are capable to reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III) [10]. The VB potential of BMO is about 2.47 eV vs. NHE, which is higher than E0 (OH−/
OH) (1.99 eV vs. NHE), implying that h+ of BMO is able to oxidize OH− to yield
OH radicals [64].
OH radicals may also be generated from the reaction between
O2 − radicals and H2O [65]. It should not be neglected that the photogenerated holes (h+) may directly oxidize some suitable substances, such as RhB [51]; the presence of which has been evidenced by the reduced RhB degradation when adding TEA (Fig. 10).

A proposed mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 2 to explain the enhanced photocatalytic performance of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2. Synergistic effects are believed between the components (CDs, GNFs and BMO) and heterojunctions in the ternary composite of C5/BMO/GNF-0.2. Under the simulated solar light irradiation, electrons (e−) are excited from VB to CB of BMO (Eq. (5)), which would then transfer to GNFs or CDs, owing to their outstanding electronic conductivity and effective contact between heterojunctions of CDs-BMO and BMO-GNFs (Eq. (7)). In addition, GNFs might act as a good charge transfer bridge, where the electrons would move freely toward its surface and be trapped by CDs that are loaded on the surfaces of GNFs. To proceed in this way, the electrons (e−) could be efficiently separated from h+; the recombination of the photogenerated carriers would be effectively reduced. The electrons (e−) show a strong ability to reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III) (Eq. (8)). The holes (h+) left at BMO, along with the generated
OH and
O2 − radicals contribute to the photodegradation of RhB, MB and phenol (Eqs. (6), (9), and (10)). Therefore, the construction of ternary CDs/BMO/GNFs composites is beneficial to improve the spatial separation of the photogenerated e− and h+ pairs, and in turn boost photocatalytic activities for both degradation of organics and reduction of Cr(VI), as evidenced in Figs. 6 and 7.
(5) BMO + hυ → e−(BMO) + h+(BMO)
(6) e−(BMO) + O2 →
O2 −
(7) e−(BMO) + CDs/GNFs → e−(CDs/GNFs)
(8) HCrO4 − + 7H++3e− → Cr(III) + 4H2O
(9) h+(BMO) + OH− →
OH
(10)
OH/
O2 −/h+(BMO) + RhB/MB/phenol → degradation products

In summary, we synthesized the ternary CDs/BMO/GNFs composites for the first time, targeting at the boosted photocatalytic activity to remove organic pollutants, i.e. RhB, MB and phenol, as well as Cr(VI). The optimized sample, C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 (the Bi/C molar ratio of 0.2 and with the addition of 5 mL CDs), showed the best photocatalytic performance, that 99.4% of RhB could be removed under the simulated solar light irradiation after 70 min. The pseudo-first-order rate constant k of RhB degradation using C5/BMO/GNF-0.2 was increased by 7.1, 4.1 and 5.4 times, as compared to those of pure BMO, BMO/GNF-0.2 and C5/BMO. The enhanced photocatalytic activity could be attributed to the efficient spatial separation of the photogenerated electrons and holes, arising from the synergistic effects between different components and heterojunctions in the ternary composite. The organic pollutants were suggested being decomposed by the photogenerated holes (h+),
OH and
O2− radicals; whilst Cr(VI) might be reduced by the photogenerated electrons (e−). This work explored the fabrication of novel ternary CDs/BMO/GNFs composites with promising photocatalytic performance; and revealed their great potential for use in practical wastewater treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge fundings from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21607064, No. 21707055 and No. 21567008), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (No. 20181BAB203018, No. 20181BAB213010) Program of 5511 Talents in Scientific and Technological Innovation of Jiangxi Province (20165BCB18014), Academic and Technical Leaders of the Main Disciplines in Jiangxi Province (20172BCB22018), and Qingjiang youth Talent program of Jiangxi University of Science and Technology (No. JXUSTQJYX20170005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2019.02.024.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
